sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE]
My mother's youngest brother, [?
Jakob-- ?] the one that we're both named after, the same great grandfather-- they had a little girl, [?
Marilka, ?] and at that time they gave her up to-- she was hidden-- they hid her in a wardrobe.
And they went down the street, left her, then they rehearsed with her what she should do.
She should be quiet.
They came back, she was gone.
They took her away.
In fact, I met this aunt after the war in Bergen-Belsen, but my fa-- I went with my-- I came with my uncle to Auschwitz together.
We were together a few days.
And they took him away, with her brother also.
I met her in 1945.
But she wasn't a relative.
Apparently she found somebody else, so she had no contact with me.
But they took away [?
Marilka, ?] like they did thousands, thousands of other children.
I was already bigger.
I was a worker.
I was 14 years old by then, so I was still useful.
These-- these things, like I said, these things were the worst.
The uncertainty, and the food, the hunger.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the worst.
I used to go to bed crying.
I was hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You cannot talk about everything.
There are things you cannot even talk about.
How you felt, what that hunger did to you.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The nice things that I remember from ghetto, I said before the party that we had, because of the start when Russia came into the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to go to concerts.
There was a theater in the ghetto.
Comics.
There were jokes.
They created jokes in the ghetto!
When the English-- they might have took Algiers-- that was, I think, in the winter of '42.
At that time it was freezing cold in the ghetto.
And at one time it rained, the-- the snow melted.
And then it rained and it froze again.
And you couldn't walk.
We walk, you sort of fell, you broke your neck.
And there was a joke going around the ghetto.
Two women, one asked, did you hear Algiers fell?
She says, I'm not surprised.
It's so slippery, why not?
So these were-- we joked about the food, about the Russians, about the Beirat.
That was like the government of the ghetto-- the Jewish government of the ghetto.
And of course Rumkowski, the president.
The money-- they printed money, with little Stars of David all over, with [INAUDIBLE].
Rumkowski signed the money.
As a matter of fact, I've got some of the money that I bought when we were in Lodz after the war in '97.
I bought a couple of bills from a merchant in Lodz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The continuous tearing away people, and finding different ways.
And then they brought in people.
They kept on replenishing from different little towns around Lodz, from everywhere.
Brought them into the factories.
We always, you know, again using that kind of logic, as long as we work and they need us, of course-- I mean, we make the furs for the soldiers that went to the Russian front.
I mean, how could they conduct a war without us?
They'll keep us until the end.
This was our logic because we wanted-- what was that?
We had-- we call-- we have a Jewish agency, [HEBREW] which means, Jews want it that way.
This was our logic.
We created our own news.
And of course there were people that had hidden radios.
Two very close friends of mine had a radio and they carried it in two little parts, separated, and they listened to the radio and we had the news continuously.
If they'd been caught, they would kill them on the spot or send to Auschwitz right away.
And it was funny to see.
One was a very prominent Jewish leader of the community in Melbourne, Australia, and the other one lives in Australia.
Very [INAUDIBLE].
They were just here visiting us.
[HEBREW] Vina was about 6'3', and [INAUDIBLE], my friend, was barely five feet.
And it was funny to see those two guys walk in the ghetto with these two parts.
And he carried half the radio and he carried half the radio.
And we had news all the time.
That's how we knew about Auschwitz, about the gas chambers.
They listened to London radio, you know, underground radios.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the final blow came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the middle of 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the German [NON-ENGLISH] of the ghetto, Hans [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he couldn't hold onto it any longer.
He wanted to hold it because as long as he held onto the ghetto, not only did he make money-- he was like a Schindler, like Oskar, but in a different way.
Not-- he didn't risk his life, quite the opposite.
He worked for the Germans, very legally.
But it saved his life.
They didn't send him to the Army.

So the factories were going until the Germans wanted to dissolve the ghetto, or maybe it was because the-- the Allies and the Russians came closer and closer and they wanted to go away further into Germany.
So around July, I think, they started dissolving the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were hiding, my mother, myself, and I think another relative.
I think it was the mother of my aunt, the one that gave away the little girl.
I don't know how come she was staying with us.
The two women hid in bed, and I think I hid under the bed or something.
They passed us by.
And then we-- we went, we were hiding on the cemetery for a few days.
Didn't even come back to sleep.
We thought we'll be able to get away with it.
But how long can you be outside, no food, no nothing.
So when we came back, they came for us.
I remember the soldiers coming up the stairs in the ghetto.
[INAUDIBLE] Street, we lived, with machine guns.
Not with the usual brutality, nothing extraordinary, nothing that scared us.
We were used to it.
And they took-- we were prepared to go.
We had a rucksack, you know, a backpack with-- I think there was a limit on how much you could carry, so we took some clothes with us.
Didn't need it anyway.
They told us we're going to go, we're going to be resettled to a different camp, work camp.
And the elderly people are going to be separate, the children are going to separate and taken care of.
The old people are going to be taken care of.

In fact, there were rumors already that some people came back and they saw the elderly people are being taking care of beautifully-- beautiful food, they don't even need to work, and the children are being taken care.
People said.
You never saw who saw it, but you heard, somebody heard from somebody that saw it.
And they took us to the jail, which was right around the corner from where we lived, [INAUDIBLE] Jail.
And I think we stayed there overnight, and then they marched us to the train station.
I don't even remember whether that was-- it was in Magazyn.
It was somewhere near the ghetto.
And they put us in the box cars.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of people was packed.
There was hardly room to sit down or anything.
[SIGHS] And they put a huge can in there.
This was supposed to the toilet, men and women together.
And they closed the-- the box cars, and we left.
I was with my mother then.
And just to show how nice the Germans are, everybody go they can of conserved pork with all the fat in it, as you going away present.
Nobody thought about-- I mean, at that time, we didn't worry about cholesterol.
So of course we ate it.
We were hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when you eat this kind of fat-- it was just fat-- this-- I don't whether everybody-- in our wagon I know we got that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody got diarrhea.
So you had that can, somewhere on the corner, or on the middle.
I don't know how long it took us to go to Birkenau, but we arrived there at dawn, because I remember it was still dark.
And when they opened the box cars, I thought I was in a circus.
Because I saw a bunch of guys coming up dressed just-- dressed in pants, these striped-- the blue-and-white striped pants, nothing on top.
Terrific bodies, you know, really like that.
That's why I thought I was in a-- shaven heads.
This was the [NON-ENGLISH], the group that worked in the crematorias and at the trains.
And they unload-- and started yelling and kicking us out and unloading us.
And everybody made sure that they had their luggage with them.
God forbid you should lose something.
And they marched us down and said, leave all the luggage here.
Your names are on it.
You'll get it back later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Men separate and women separate.
Separated.
I managed to see where my mother was.
And then I saw Dr.
Mengele.
Then I didn't know who he was, and he was standing there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They must've separated the women-- he must've worked on the women first, because I remember I saw my mother go to the other side.
And I knew what the other side was because I saw that the young people-- the ones that looked a little better went to the yards, the working people.
My mother went to the other side.
Now let's go back to the ghetto, talking about my mother.
Four weeks before being sent out to Auschwitz, I became ill.
I had terrible pain.
I had a-- a hernia.
Went to the doctor in the hospital, and he said, I think we need to operate.
And I-- at that time, I was already 16 years old.
It was 1944.
Yeah, 17.
I asked my mother whether I should do it.
She said, [INAUDIBLE], you're a grown up man.
You must decide this for yourself.
Luckily, I decided to have it done.
They operated on me only with a local anesthetic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The pain was terrible.
I had four injections.
And then they had to wait because they were boiling the gloves.
They didn't have new gloves.
They were boiling the gloves.
And the doctor came over, and they started operating.
And there was one moment where they attached something that gives you a terrible pain in the chest.
I thought they were tearing me apart, and I started yelling and screaming.
And I remember the doctor saying in Polish to the assistant, you know what?
Why don't we leave him here until tomorrow until he shuts up and stops screaming.
They were making a joke.
Of course they wouldn't do it, but it was a joke for them.
Why I'm talking about the surgery-- it's not such an important thing in my life-- is because of the visits of my mother.
And I have the pictures still in my mind.
My mother came to see me.
At that time, my mother was 46 years old.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There are 95-year-old women now that looked better than she did.
Her face was like that-- swollen.
Her legs were like that-- swollen from hunger.
Her eyes were so puffed up.
I cried when she left because of the way she looked, at 45 or 46.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is how I remember her, because if this was, say, in June or July 1944, I was with her maybe another month, that's all.
And I saw Dr.
Mengele sending her on the other side the selection, and all I saw was her walking away, hunched.
That's all.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nevertheless, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was so happy when they sent me to the good side.
Can you imagine that?
I just saw my mother being taken away and I knew where they were taking her.
Nevertheless, I was very happy that I went to the other side.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I got to live with that.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] That's why there are plenty of moments when I don't think I was that lucky that I survived.
Because if you have to carry a package like that with you, it wasn't worth it.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They marched us to one of the camps, I don't know, in Birkenau.
And there was one guy-- he had a special nickname, I forgot about it.
He was the speaker.
He told us what we should do.
Give up the gold.
If you've got gold teeth, tell us.
We're going to pull them out.
If you swallowed anything, we'll take it out of you.
So you better give it up right now.
If you swallowed anything, you know what to do.
Because what we'll have to do will be a lot worse.
He was a Jewish guy, and-- one of the kapos, I guess.
And we got undressed.
They shaved us and put the entlausen, you know, against lice-- you know, the disinfectant, which hurt like hell after they scratched you and everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- I came with a group.
In fact, my uncle was there too, the Uncle [?
Jacob. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They marched us into Birkenau into the gypsy camp.
This was called the gypsy camp.
You know, Birkenau is different to Auschwitz.
Birkenau were the long barracks where, before the war, the Polish Calvary kept their horses.
So in the middle of the barrack was a long trough, and we were lying on the floor on both sides on the concrete.
And I remember the blankets they used to give us to lie on, with the long hair, full of fleas and lice.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember how there-- whether I was in a-- I must've been in a different barrack, because later they sent me to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] number 22, where the children, where the youngsters.
There were maybe a thousand kids in that barrack.
There was one Hungarian, the-- the deputy blockalteste, that gave the-- the command in the evening.
Everybody lie down like-- like sardines, like that.
Turn over!
So everybody had to turn over together.
And if you had to go to the bathroom during the night-- there was a huge can standing outside-- you couldn't get back to your own place because you were always walking on people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One particular incident I remember, before they sent me out from Birkenau-- and this again had to do with my revolutionary upbringing of not standing for any injustices.
I was among the oldest in that block of the kids, and I saw how they gave us the food.
The food came in.
We used to get a loaf of bread for four people.
Small loaf.
It was hard like a rock.
So how do you cut the loaf in four?
Like that, right?
The margarine came in.
A huge block was already cut up into little blocks.
All they needed to do was take off that little block, put it on you.
They cut a loaf-- I'm talking about the Jewish, the kapos, and the other kapos-- they brought in the bread, and they cut it up into six or five.
Cutted a chunk out of the middle, and then again.
So divided the bread instead into four, after the meager ration that the Germans gave us, they stole.
The margarine that came in already cut up to-- so that they can't take off the portion, they just slumped it all together, just took with a little knife, a little piece.
And I watched that and my blood was boiling.
Hey that's not right.
So I took a couple of guys and I go to the front of the camp in Birkenau, of the gypsy camp, to the Jewish kapos.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I tell them about the injustice as if I was the first one in 1944 that discovered injustice in Auschwitz.
Oh, they were very-- oh, they were very upset about it.
He said, let's go, we're going to teach him a lesson.
The blockalteste, the Kommandant of our block, was a Pollack from a little town called Pabianice.
Why he was in camp, I don't know.
He was there, either a criminal, or they just put him in camp.
His deputy was a Jewish guy, Volkovich.
I still remember.
He was about 30, 33.
He was already an old man.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Now these guys came back with me and with the group that came to report that thing.
They called them out and they said, aren't you ashamed of yourselves, stealing from kids and all that?
And they walked away.
Now that guy, Volkovich, just looked at me and said to me, I'll pay you for that.
I had a belt that I brought with me from ghetto.
I don't remember where I got that.
It was a Polish officer's belt, a leather belt.
It was about that wide.
And the buckle, with thick buckle, you know, officers wear.
When they left, I didn't manage to run away.
When they left, he grabbed me, pulled off my belt.
I don't know how I was still left alive.
He beat the hell-- I was bleeding from everywhere.
Finally, a couple of guys took me away.
He-- he was raging.
He was-- he nearly got a heart attack, he was so furious.
Of course, I didn't achieve anything except the beating I got.
And they hid me in another barracks so he didn't see me for a few days.
Now, I met this guy after the war, in Lodz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1945, I was in Lodz after I came back from Auschwitz.
It was in March or April, and it was in front of the Jewish Committee, and he walks past me.
And here I see a policeman, I mean he-- as if he came out from under me.
And I jumped on top of him and I told the police who he was.
Of course, they arrested him right away.
At that time, they kept on arresting people right and left.
And they put him in jail.
And they called me.
He took my name.
They called me.
They kept on calling me back about two or three times.
And the officer, a Pole, also came out of concentration camp.
He said to me, Jack, I got to have witnesses.
I believe what you say.
If you have two witnesses, I'm going to hang this guy right here.
But I have to have witnesses.
There were a couple guys that were there that didn't want to go.
They said, after the war, he's a Jewish guy.
We suffered enough.
Nah, we don't want to go.
So finally, he told me-- he called me in that office and said, look Jack.
You don't have any witnesses today.
He's gone.
I've got to let him go.
And he let him go.
But we'll come back to him later in Australia.
I met him in Australia.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When these guys took me out-- when they hid me in a different barrack-- we still stayed in Birkenau for a while.
I think in Birkenau I stayed altogether maybe two weeks, three weeks.
And then they picked out a group and they tattooed me, which meant-- and they gave me a uniform, that black and white.
You know, the striped uniform with the hat and all that.
I was very proud.
I'm-- I'm one of the elite.
I'm wearing a uniform, and I already have a number on my hand.
And in a short while, they put us on trucks and sent us to the camp, another concentration camp.
That here is my number-- B10237.
You know it's funny.
I was born the 13th, and everything I do comes up with a 13.
And my numbers, totaled up, also make 13.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They sent us, not very far from a-- from a camp that belonged-- was under the jurisdiction of Auschwitz.
The camp was called Trzebinia.
There was a bombed oil refinery there.
They had oil refineries there before the war, and it was bombed.
And what we did there, we build it up.
The huge oil refineries, you built with bricks, up, like a wall around it.
Maybe a foot away from the-- from the the steel refinery, they built bricks around it.
This was our job.
And then we also built-- there was one job that was called [GERMAN].
We called-- we built a bunker for the Germans.
I think it was a 35-foot ceiling poured with concrete.
I remember I was running with the-- with a wheelbarrow full with mixed concrete, on a board maybe that wide, running, that was slippery from the snow.
You'd be surprised the things you can do when you got a guy with a rifle behind you, and the rifle's pointed at you.
You can perform miracles which otherwise you couldn't do.
This camp had about 800 to 1,000 people.
When we came, there were already some people there from Hungary-- there were some Hungarian Jews-- and some from Kielce, from a city called Kielce.
It's a big city in Poland.
We were the last group that they brought there.
The Kommandant of this camp was a Pole, a German Pole.
Before the war, he was an officer in the Polish Calvary.
Now he was an SS man.
His name was Kovel.
In fact, I met people here that were under him in a different camp, and they knew what a brutal monster that was.

He managed, in a small camp like that, to have his private crematorium to burn the people that died-- no, over there they didn't have a gas chamber-- so that he doesn't have to transport them or bury them.
His own crematorium, he had, for one person.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I worked there, that was in August.
I worked there until January, '45.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was very scared.
I don't know why I used to get slapped every day.
The Komman-- the-- what was he called?
The lager alteste.
He was also a prisoner.
He was a murderer.
Sent him to-- a German, sent to prison.
So he was our Kommandant in-- inside the camp.
And there was an [? oberkapo, ?] [INAUDIBLE], I remember.
Head like that, like the moon, also a murderer.
They were convict to life convictions.
So they were our leaders.
I don't know how-- wherever he walked past in the camp, I was next to him.
I don't know how I managed to do that.
And every time, he slapped me.
It got so that if I didn't get a smack for him, I was cold.
I couldn't sleep.
And if he hit me, I was right on the ground.
He had-- this guy was eating.
I wasn't.
One time I remember looking in a-- we didn't have mirrors.
I mean, I didn't need a mirror.
I didn't shave then.
I didn't have to groom myself.
There's no hair, nothing.
But once I looked in a mirror in a-- in a window, and I saw myself and I started to cry.
My face was burned.
I burned very dark.
And it was red from the sun.
I looked like a monster.
I got so scared, I cried.
I cried my eyes out.
It scared me so much.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once I was punished in the camp.
I was afraid to steal.
I knew that if I steal and they catch me, they'll kill me.
We were out at the [NON-ENGLISH] at work once, and a couple guys came over with pockets full of potatoes.
There was a kitchen right there, said, and there's a cellar on the side.
And nobody is there.
There was not one German.
Go get some.
They were-- they all had pockets full of potatoes.
I didn't want to go, but you know, the temptation, you were hungry.
So I went.
And sure enough, I came out of the cellar.
I go up, there was a little hill, and there was a German standing with-- with a rifle like that.
He didn't do anything to me.
He just took my number.
Number-- we had the number here, I think, on the pants.
He didn't have to tell me to o-- to show him the tattoo.
And sure enough, we didn't-- nothing happened all day.
When we came home from work, we had an appel.
You had to stand out for an hour, whatever they felt like, before we got our dinner, the soup.
They called my number.
And I get up.
This one was a very clever one.
It was under a cold shower.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About two of them were beating me with these rubber hoses.
They had to carry-- I don't remember what happened then because they carried me.
Another thing from Trzebinia, from the camp.
The very-- the first few days I was there, I had a high temperature.
My temperature was raised.
And this was an indication of lung problems.
And we thought TB or whatever.
So I went to the infirmary.
I went to the doctor.
We had a Hungarian doctor there, Hungarian Jew.
Also a prisoner.
He took my temperature.
He said, you know what?
I'll leave you on the camp.
Not in the hospital-- block [GERMAN], it was called.
You were left in the barracks.
You didn't go to work.
The reason I remember this is because of what we had there.
Our camp Kommandant, Koven-- Kovel had an enclosure for geese.
And there was one boy-- he was called a [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know what that means.
It was-- why-- his protege, boy from Kielce.
A Jewish boy also, prisoner.
And he looks good.
A lot of food.
And I befriended this guy.
He took care of the geese.
The key geese had to fed.
What do they feed them?
They collect-- I don't know where, for-- from a bakery-- old stale bread and challah and rolls and all that.
So they had two sacks that high-- you know, we're talking about 100-pound bags-- with bread, with stale-- it was green already-- two huge sacks for the geese.
And he always took a piece of-- and this guy told me about it.
And he said, when I was left in the camp for the block [GERMAN] when I had this high temperature-- I think I was left there three days.
I had a ball.
I had a go at this bread.
Well, apparently the other guys found out too.
And within a week, these two sacks were empty.
That Kommandant nearly went crazy.
He wanted to kill us all.
But they found different bread.
But I don't think there was any general punishment for that.
But what happened, I ate myself sick to such a-- I had bread, the stale bread, under my-- on my bed, when I slept, you know, on the-- on the bunks.
I put it on the-- you know, we had these-- was it straw or-- or wood shavings we slept on, a sack with wood shaving.
